kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Friday 10-Jul OFFICIAL posted (Westmetall, verified Sun): $3,156.00 vs 09-Jul $3,164.00 = −$8.00 (−0.25%) — the five-day streak snaps, but gently: officials caught only half the evening fade ($3,139.5 close, −1.91%). Saturday's re-escalation (IRGC fires on a Hormuz transit, declares the strait closed) sets Monday's gap-risk HIGHER. ~18.1% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 10-Jul official $3,153.00 (−$16.00, −0.50%) — and the cash–3M spread FLIPPED to +$3.00 BACKWARDATION (from −$5 contango), the first positive front spread since the June war-premium regime. Friday's later closing print was $3,139.5 (−1.91%), right on the .50 retrace of the $3,065→$3,210.5 bounce; $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count, $3,050 re-opens the downside.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 10-Jul official 287,725 t (Westmetall) — a further −1,500 t draw, extending the unbroken 2026 slide (509kt on 02-Jan → 288kt, −43.5% YTD); ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.2 days of global output. Draws persisted through escalation, stand-down and re-escalation alike — the structural-deficit signal stands, and the spread just flipped to back it.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Friday 10-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall, verified Sun 12-Jul): $3,156.00 vs 09-Jul $3,164.00 = −$8.00 (−0.25%) — the five-session official streak snaps, though cash held the $3,150 shelf while the evening close fell harder ($3,139.5, −1.91%). Saturday's Hormuz re-escalation makes Monday's official the next arbiter. ~18.1% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 10-Jul official $3,153.00 vs 09-Jul $3,169.00 (−$16.00, −0.50%). Officials (13:20 London) sit ABOVE the confirmed Friday closing price ($3,139.5, −1.91%) — the fade accelerated into the evening. The .50 retrace of the $3,065→$3,210.5 bounce ($3,138) held on a closing basis; $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline) upgrades the count, $3,050 re-opens the downside. avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: FLIP TO BACKWARDATION: 10-Jul official cash $3,156.00 vs 3M $3,153.00 = +$3.00 — the first positive front spread since the June war-premium regime, arriving with stock at 287,725 t (13th straight week of draws) and BEFORE the weekend's re-escalation. Yesterday's framing stands: a flip to backwardation flags the war-premium regime returning — it just printed. Watch Monday for confirmation vs a one-day artefact.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: Latest public print remains the 09-Jul night close ¥23,105 (+0.20%, SMM) ≈ $3,019 excl-VAT; Chinese markets were closed over the weekend. SHFE/LME ratio repaired to 7.36 with four-year-high destocking underneath; Monday's session (plus June trade data due Mon 13-Jul) will price Saturday's Hormuz escalation. avg = June mean (SMM).

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t, Platts-assessed 06-Jul — the highest quarterly Japan premium in a decade (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). NOTE: spot has now fallen BELOW the settlement (SMM MJP spot $355, 10-Jul) — Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor but a softening spot tape. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: FRESH SMM weekly print (10-Jul): Japan MJP ingot spot premium PLUNGED to $355/t from $380 last week — buyers restocking only for immediate needs, tight-supply expectations easing, and traders arbitraging spot vs long-term contracts. First spot print below the $395 Q3 settlement — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts daily P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last assessed $525–560/t (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul — no fresher public Platts print at run time; SMM's 10-Jul weekly flags overseas premiums broadly lower into the off-season. The 6% duty + CBAM + Mozal's care-and-maintenance keep the structural floor (S&P Global).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs dp Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print at run time); conversion premia stay stickier than ingot premia and Mozal (~580ktpa) remains on care & maintenance — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul; prev public print 119.05¢ on 20-May) — the tariff-$ leg moves mechanically with the LME (≈$1,582 at the 09-Jul cash $3,164). SMM's 10-Jul weekly confirms the drift lower (DDP 110.25→109.75¢) — verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: FRESH SMM weekly print (10-Jul): US Midwest DDP premium eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t from 110.25¢ (≈$2,431) on 03-Jul — off-season demand, looser-supply expectations for H2 (restarts + new capacity) and cautious buying. ~2/3 of the all-in value is the 50% Section-232 tariff leg — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0020.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: Week-end snapshot: DXY finished the week around 100.5–101 — TE tracked late-week easing as stand-down headlines sapped haven demand, while Fortune notes the index ended the WEEK higher after the mid-week strike-spike; no fresh weekend print. Saturday's re-escalation argues USD-supportive risk-off into Monday. Fed framework review (Chair Warsh's five working groups) still de-emphasises forward guidance (TE / Fortune / CME).

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Closed the week near 1.1420. ECB June minutes: the energy shock can no longer be ignored — the Council unanimously raised the key rate to 2.25% in June, the first major central bank to tighten on war-driven energy costs; a renewed Gulf flare keeps that energy-inflation channel live (FXStreet / ECB / SMM).

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.77 into the weekend. SHFE/LME price ratio repaired to 7.36; import window still shut. June CPI +1.0% / PPI +4.1% keeps easing in play; June trade data (incl. unwrought aluminium exports) due Mon 13-Jul (SMM / NBS / customs calendar).

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen firmed to 161.70 Friday (−0.42%) on reports Japan may steer major pension funds toward domestic assets. A still-weak yen marginally supports Japan-bound cargo premia — though MJP spot fell to $355 anyway; Saturday's Hormuz closure declaration is the bigger Q4-talks variable (Investing.com / TE / SMM).

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: Closed the week at 4.56% after easing from Wednesday's 4.59% high as oil retreated. CME FedWatch (via SMM 10-Jul): July hold 74.9%; September cumulative-hike odds ~64% (51.1% one hike + 13.1% two). A weekend oil gap would re-tighten the rates ceiling (Advisor Perspectives / CME / SMM).

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Brent SETTLED $76.01 Friday (−$0.29, −0.38%) — but rose ~4% on the WEEK on the mid-week strike exchange (Fortune, 10-Jul). Saturday's ship attack + strait-closure declaration is the classic setup for a higher Monday open; energy remains the cost-side transmission into smelter power (Fortune / TE).

macro.name: WTI crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: WTI tracked Brent into the $72s at Friday's close, also up ~4% on the week. The 08-Jul re-imposition of comprehensive US oil sanctions on Iran (wind-down to 17-Jul) keeps a floor under crude even between strike headlines (Fortune / CNBC / TE).

macro.name: Natural gas ($/MMBtu)
macro.note: US Henry Hub ~$3.28 on record LNG feedgas; EU TTF spiked to €47.97/MWh on the 07-Jul Qatari LNG-carrier attack and stays the marginal cost channel for European smelter power — a renewed Gulf flare feeds straight into this leg (Reuters / TE).

macro_events: FRIDAY OFFICIALS POSTED — STREAK SNAPS, SPREAD FLIPS: Al cash $3,156.00 (−0.25%) / 3M $3,153.00 (−0.50%) end five straight official gains; cash–3M flips to +$3.00 BACKWARDATION (first since June's war-premium regime); stock 287,725 t (−1,500, −43.5% YTD). Officials sit above the weaker evening close ($3,139.5, −1.91%) — and all of it pre-dates Saturday (Westmetall, verified Sun; lme.com).

WEEKEND RE-ESCALATION (Sat 11-Jul): the IRGC fired on / hit a merchant ship attempting the Hormuz transit and DECLARED THE STRAIT CLOSED; the US launched retaliatory strikes on dozens of Iranian targets; Khamenei and Trump exchanged public threats. Pre-war, the Gulf supplied ~9% of global aluminium and ~25% of ex-China supply — the war-premium re-price is Monday's opening trade (CNN live / Washington Post / CBS, 11-Jul).

THE DIPLOMATIC TRACK SURVIVES — SO FAR: Araghchi met Oman's FM Badr Albusaidi in Muscat on a safe-passage mechanism for commercial shipping (Art. 5, Islamabad MoU); US officials expect Tehran to issue a statement declaring the strait OPEN, and a senior US official says nuclear-track negotiations will 'never' begin without free tanker transit; Araghchi counters that the US Treasury is violating Para 9. Treasury's Iranian-oil wind-down closes 17-Jul — the week's hard date (The National / MEE / CNN).

OIL & RATES INTO THE WEEKEND: Brent settled $76.01 (−0.38% Friday, +4% on the week); US 10Y closed 4.56%; DXY ended ~100.5–101 with the week's whipsaw; CME FedWatch: July hold 74.9%, September cumulative-hike ~64%. US June CPI lands Tuesday 14-Jul — the rates leg re-prices twice this week: once on CPI, once on oil's reaction to the strait (Fortune / Advisor Perspectives / CME via SMM).

SUPPLY REPAIR vs WAR RISK: EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah alumina refinery RESTARTED (10-Jul; ~50% within days, full capability end-2026; smelter rebuild ahead of schedule, 89 cells back) — the clearest supply-return evidence yet, now hostage to the same strait the weekend re-contested (EGA / Argus / The National).

OVERSEAS PREMIUMS BROADLY LOWER (SMM weekly, 10-Jul, pre-escalation): Japan MJP spot $380→$355 (below the $395 Q3 settlement); CIF Korea $350→$319; FCA Korea $366→$335; CIF Thailand $345→$335; US MW DDP 110.25→109.75¢/lb. Friday's next weekly print shows whether Saturday reverses the slide (SMM).

CHINA: destocking at a four-year-high pace with the SHFE/LME ratio at 7.36; markets closed over the weekend — Monday's session prices the escalation alongside June trade data (aluminium exports running +12–18% YoY). Scrap tightening: Shandong suspends reverse invoicing from July; UAE scrap-export ban; EU tariff hike (SMM / customs).

FREIGHT & WEEK AHEAD: BDI 2,944 (+34 Friday, one-month high, demand-led) — Gulf war-risk quotes likely RE-WIDEN after the ship attack. Q2 season: Alcoa Thu 16-Jul (after NYSE close), Rio Tinto Q2 production mid-week, Norsk Hydro 22-Jul; Zhongfu's H1 alert (+154–176%) already sets the China tone (Hellenic Shipping News / BusinessWire / SMM).
lme_commentary: The week's last officials settle Friday's argument: cash $3,156.00 (−0.25%) and 3M $3,153.00 (−0.50%) snap the five-session streak — but the real signal is the spread, which flipped to +$3.00 BACKWARDATION (from −$5) with stock down again to 287,725 t. The evening closing price faded harder ($3,139.5, −1.91%), tagging the .50 retrace of the $3,065→$3,210.5 bounce — a clean wave-ii pullback — and then Saturday rewrote the driver stack: the IRGC fired on a merchant ship attempting the Hormuz transit and declared the strait CLOSED, drawing US retaliatory strikes on dozens of targets even as Araghchi sat down in Muscat to negotiate a safe-passage mechanism. Monday therefore opens with the front spread, the stock ledger and the news cycle all pointing the same way for the first time since early June — gap-up risk into the $3,230 (MA20) / $3,260 (.382) resistance stack, with $3,050 the structural line below. The one bearish anchor: every overseas premium print of the past week said the physical market outside China is adequately supplied — escalation has to overcome an off-season tape, not a tight one.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference (no fresh public print at run time); LME's Platts-settled alumina contract closed $330 (02-Jul, Alcircle); oversupply now DEEPENS with EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarting (10-Jul); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 — verify SMM/Fastmarkets

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304 — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference); EGA's refinery return adds ~2.4 Mtpa back to ex-China supply through H2 — verify Platts

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public print ¥2,716 (08-Jul, +0.37% short-covering bounce); price below ALL moving averages with the bearish alignment intact; port stocks building on high ex-China arrivals; Chinese markets closed over the weekend — Monday reopens with the EGA-restart surplus vs the Hormuz re-escalation pulling in opposite directions (SMM)

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309.4 — CME/TradingView (unchanged public print at run time)

alumina_notes: THE BIG ONE: EGA restarted alumina production at Al Taweelah (announced 10-Jul) — output to ~50% of capacity within days and full technical capability by end-2026. The 2.4 Mtpa refinery (≈half of EGA's alumina needs) had been down since the 28-Mar drone strike on KEZAD; its return deepens the ex-China surplus and removes a tail-risk bid from the seaborne market (EGA/Reuters/The National).

China: SHFE alumina (AO2609, last public print ¥2,716) sits below all moving averages with port stocks building on heavy ex-China arrivals; SMM still expects weak consolidation. Guinea's pending bauxite quota policy remains the only clear bullish catalyst; the Guinea–EGA settlement keeps existing flows normalised. India adds length: MECON completed trial runs of conveyor systems for NALCO's refinery expansion (10-Jul).

Gulf logistics risk is easing but live: ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz — the stand-down and resumed talks reduce the war-risk bid on Gulf-bound alumina/caustic freight, but surcharges stay elevated until the Oman track proves durable.
net_read: THE SPREAD SPOKE FIRST. Friday's officials snapped the streak on flat price (cash $3,156, 3M $3,153) but flipped the front to +$3 backwardation on a 287,725 t stock base — physical tightening, printed BEFORE the IRGC declared the strait closed on Saturday. The market now opens the week with fear and physical pointing the same way while the premium tape (every overseas spot mark lower, assessed pre-escalation) argues the off-season cushion is real. Net: upside risk dominates near-term ($3,230/$3,260 the test), but the move is headline-fragile — an Iranian 'strait open' statement out of the Muscat track reverses it as fast as it came.

feed.text: FRIDAY OFFICIALS POSTED (Westmetall, verified Sunday): Al cash $3,156.00 (−0.25%) / 3M $3,153.00 (−0.50%) — the five-day streak snaps — but the cash–3M spread FLIPS to +$3.00 backwardation (first positive front spread since June's war-premium regime) and stock draws again to 287,725 t. Base complex: Cu $13,454 (+0.3%), Ni $16,600 (+0.4%), Zn $3,588 (−0.7%), Pb $1,890 (09-Jul, −0.3%).

RE-ESCALATION: the IRGC fired on / hit a merchant ship attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz and DECLARED THE STRAIT CLOSED; the US answered with retaliatory strikes on dozens of Iranian targets — the second weekend flare in a row, this one striking directly at the transit artery for ~9% of global aluminium supply (CNN live / Washington Post / CBS, 11-Jul). Price-bullish via the war-premium re-price.

MUSCAT TRACK STILL LIVE: FM Araghchi met Oman's FM Badr Albusaidi on mechanisms for safe passage of commercial vessels through Hormuz (Art. 5 of the Islamabad MoU); US officials say they expect Tehran to issue a statement declaring the strait OPEN, and a senior US official says nuclear-track talks will 'never' start unless tankers transit freely. Araghchi accuses the US Treasury of violating Para 9 (The National / Middle East Eye / CNN, 11-Jul).

EGA RESTARTED THE AL TAWEELAH ALUMINA REFINERY: production resumed ~3 months after the 28-Mar drone strike — ~50% of the 2.4 Mtpa capacity within days, full capability by end-2026; smelter rebuild ahead of schedule (89 cells restarted; pre-incident output targeted within a year). Ex-China alumina surplus deepens (EGA / Argus / The National).

SMM WEEKLY PREMIUMS: overseas spot premiums broadly LOWER — Japan MJP spot $380→$355 (first print below the $395 Q3 settlement), CIF Korea $350→$319, FCA Korea $366→$335, CIF Thailand $345→$335, US MW DDP 110.25→109.75¢/lb — off-season demand, fading tight-supply expectations, spot-vs-contract arbitrage (SMM). Saturday's escalation now tests how much of that slide survives.

FREIGHT: BDI +34 to 2,944 — fresh one-month high, demand-led (iron ore / coal / bauxite; Capesize strength). Gulf war-risk quotes had been stabilising on the stand-down — expect RE-WIDENING after Saturday's ship attack (Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News).

CHINA Q2-SEASON OPENER: Zhongfu Industrial guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154–176% YoY, crediting industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices at highs, high-value-added products and energy-saving retrofits — an early public read on Chinese producer margins into results season (SMM).

news.headline: HOSTILITIES RESUME: a day after the 'stand down', the IRGC fired on a merchant ship transiting Hormuz and DECLARED THE STRAIT CLOSED; the US struck dozens of Iranian targets in response, and Iran's supreme leader and President Trump traded public threats. Talks continue anyway — Araghchi met Oman's FM in Muscat on a safe-passage mechanism, Washington wants a public Iranian declaration that the strait is open, and a senior US official conditions the entire nuclear track on free tanker transit. Next hard date: Treasury's Iranian-oil wind-down closes 17-Jul.
news.source: CNN live updates (11-Jul) — cnn.com/2026/07/11/world/live-news/iran-war-trump; Washington Post (11-Jul); CBS News; The National; Middle East Eye

news.headline: STREAK SNAPS, SPREAD FLIPS: Friday's officials printed cash $3,156 (−0.25%) / 3M $3,153 (−0.50%) — ending five straight official gains — while the front spread flipped to +$3 BACKWARDATION and stock drew to 287,725 t (−43.5% YTD). The evening close was weaker ($3,139.5, −1.91%, on the .50 retrace of the $3,065→$3,210.5 bounce) — but all of Friday's tape pre-dates Saturday's escalation. Monday opens with gap-up risk; $3,260 (.382) remains the technical trigger, $3,050 the line.
news.source: Westmetall officials (10-Jul, verified 12-Jul) — westmetall.com; LME closing prices — lme.com; SMM morning briefing (10-Jul)

news.headline: EGA RESTARTS AL TAWEELAH ALUMINA REFINERY (10-Jul): output to ~50% of the 2.4 Mtpa capacity within days, full technical capability by end-2026; the smelter rebuild runs AHEAD of schedule (89 reduction cells back; pre-incident production targeted within a year). The Gulf supply-return story keeps hard evidence — but Saturday's strikes are a reminder the repair path itself carries war risk.
news.source: EGA media release; Argus Media; The National (10-Jul) — thenationalnews.com/business/economy/2026/07/10/ega-restarts-production-at-al-taweelah-alumina-refinery/

news.headline: OVERSEAS PREMIUMS BROADLY LOWER (SMM weekly, 10-Jul): Japan MJP spot $380→$355 — first print BELOW the decade-high $395 Q3 settlement — Korea CIF $319, Thailand $335, US MW DDP 109.75¢/lb. Off-season demand, fading tight-supply fears and spot-vs-contract arbitrage did the work — all assessed BEFORE Saturday's strait closure declaration; renewed Gulf risk is the one force that re-floats these marks. Q4 MJP talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a now-contested tape.
news.source: SMM — Weekly Overseas Primary Aluminum Premiums (10-Jul) — news.metal.com/en/newscontent/103999648

news.headline: DESTOCKING HOLDS THE FLOOR: aluminium ingot warehouse withdrawals ran at a four-year high last week; the SHFE/LME ratio repaired to 7.36; the last SHFE print (¥23,105 night close, +0.20%) came with bullish position-building. Scrap keeps TIGHTENING — Shandong suspends reverse invoicing from July, the UAE bans scrap exports, the EU hikes tariffs. June trade data lands Monday — watch aluminium exports (+12–18% YoY pace) for the ex-China supply leg.
news.source: SMM morning briefing (10-Jul) — news.metal.com/en/newscontent/103997222; SMM scrap weekly

news.headline: Q2 SEASON OPENS: Alcoa reports Thursday 16-Jul after the NYSE close (call 5pm EDT) — the first Western read on tariff-era, war-premium margins; Rio Tinto's Q2 production report lands the same week and Norsk Hydro follows 22-Jul. China's tone is already set: Zhongfu Industrial pre-announced H1 profit +154–176% YoY. US June CPI (Tue) and the 17-Jul Iran deadline frame the macro.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio (Boddington, Worsley, MRN, Alumar, South Africa ops) for $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~$1.0bn stock; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 — making Alcoa the world's largest bauxite miner. Q2 results CONFIRMED for Thu 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT — BusinessWire).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 12-Jul. Q2 season opens this week: Alcoa Thu 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT — BusinessWire), Rio Tinto Q2 production report mid-week (H1 results 13-Aug), Norsk Hydro 22-Jul (07:00 CEST — GlobeNewswire), Maaden (1211) H1 late-Jul/early-Aug (Tadawul). First China datapoint already in: Zhongfu Industrial's H1 profit alert (+154–176% YoY, SMM). Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 bauxite/alumina/aluminium deal — $4.1bn upfront (up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 (announced 01-Jul). Context for the prints: Q2 average LME cash ≈ $3,459 (June) vs $3,156 now — sequentially strong realised prices with a fading tail; watch what Alcoa says about US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% tariff and about Gulf-region insurance/freight costs.

commercial.text: The weekend flipped the tactical map: Friday's wave-ii pullback ($3,153 official / $3,139.5 close, on the .50 of the bounce) now has a wave-iii catalyst behind it (strait declared closed) and a backwardation flip beneath it. Treat $3,120–150 as the accumulation shelf rather than the decision zone; resistance stack $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382), whose closing break opens $3,400. Avoid selling into Monday's gap; a gap-and-fade close below $3,150 is the signal to re-hedge. $3,050 remains the structural stop.

Last week's premium slide (MJP spot $355 vs $395 settled; Korea $319; US MW DDP 109.75¢) was assessed BEFORE the closure declaration — do not extrapolate it into Q4 assumptions this week. Hold the settled $395 Q3 anchor on Asian exposure; defer any Q4 concessions until the 17-Jul weekly prints show whether escalation arrests the slide. In the US, the tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,578) mechanically lifts the all-in with any LME gap — re-run all-in economics off Monday's print, not Friday's.

Alumina stays a buyer's market on the EGA restart (2.4 Mtpa returning; FOB Australia ~$340, API ~$304) — but the weekend re-injects Gulf risk into reagent and alumina FREIGHT. Separate commodity price from delivered cost: extend caustic/CPC cover on freight-inclusive terms while the demand-led BDI (2,944) and re-widening war-risk surcharges both push the logistics leg up. Guinea's quota push (>$100/dmt bauxite ambition) remains the cost-curve tail risk.

Act as if war-risk re-prices Monday: re-confirm Gulf war-risk cover and quote validity windows, re-validate land-bridge (Gulf→Red Sea) carrier capacity, and review force-majeure/demurrage language on any Gulf-loading fixture BEFORE booking. The strait went from 'reopening' to 'declared closed' in 24 hours — contract optionality (CIF/FOB switches, laycan flexibility) is again worth paying for.

bottom_line: On 12-Jul the ledger and the map disagree less than they have all month. Friday's officials (cash $3,156, 3M $3,153, stock 287,725 t) ended the five-day streak but flipped the front spread to backwardation — the classic deficit signature — and 24 hours later the IRGC fired on a Hormuz transit and declared the strait closed, with US strikes in reply and the Muscat safe-passage track the only thread holding the de-escalation story together. Every overseas premium fell last week and EGA's alumina refinery restarted — the repair evidence is real — but all of it was priced before Saturday. The week ahead is binary and dated: an Iranian open-strait statement (US demand), CPI Tuesday, Alcoa Thursday, the Treasury oil deadline Friday. Until one of those lands, the backwardation plus sub-288kt stock argues the tape trades from the long side, and $3,260 on a closing basis remains the number that turns a headline bounce into a trend.

forward_path.basis: Friday official (Westmetall, verified Sun 12-Jul); −0.25% vs 09-Jul — the five-day streak snaps gently, holding the $3,150 shelf. All of Friday's tape pre-dates Saturday's strait-closure declaration.
Friday official; −0.50% vs 09-Jul; the front spread FLIPPED to +$3.00 backwardation. Friday's later closing print was $3,139.5 (−1.91%) — the evening faded harder than the official.

outlook.curve_note: The front end FLIPPED TO BACKWARDATION on Friday's officials (+$3.00 cash-over-3M, from −$5 contango) with stock at 287,725 t and drawing daily — yesterday's marker ('a flip to backwardation would flag the war-premium regime returning') printed within 24 hours of the IRGC declaring the strait closed. The curve has swung back from pricing repair-vs-deficit to pricing escalation-vs-deficit: nearby dates are supported by physical tightness AND fear, while the deferred curve still embeds supply normalisation (EGA restart, China exports +12–18%, Indonesia ramp). The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) is being re-tested from below with the structural deficit (~−365 kt) as the floor — how Monday's officials treat the backwardation is the single most informative print of the week.

ai_analysis: What changed: the driver rotated BACK. Friday's tape priced repair (streak snapped, premiums sliding, EGA restarting) — then Saturday's IRGC attack on a merchant transit and declared strait closure re-armed the war premium on a market whose officials had, hours earlier, flipped the front spread to backwardation. Escalation is again the marginal price-setter; the deficit leg (sub-288kt stock, four-year-high China destocking) never left.

Term structure: the +$3.00 backwardation is the tell. It printed on Friday's officials — BEFORE the weekend — meaning the front tightened on physical draw, not fear. If Monday widens it on closure headlines, the market is stacking a fear premium on top of a physical one; if Monday restores contango, Friday was an artefact. Either way, spreads are now more informative than flat price.

Physical divergence persists: Japan's spot premium ($355) sits BELOW its own settled quarterly ($395) and every overseas spot mark fell last week — all assessed pre-escalation. Premiums are the slowest leg to re-price; if the strait stays contested, expect the next weekly prints (17-Jul) to arrest the slide, and treat any premium strength as confirmation the physical market believes the closure.

Positioning read: the last visible flows were bears exiting (LME OI −1,200 lots into Thursday's surge; SHFE bullish position-building +3,146 lots) — the rebound was short-covering plus tentative length, and weekend risk was cheap to carry. A daily close through $3,260 (.382) remains the systematic re-entry trigger; a gap-and-fade Monday that closes below $3,150 would instead validate the bear's 'sell the headline' template.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled and Platts-assessed at $395/t CIF Japan (06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). But SMM's 10-Jul weekly shows SPOT plunging to $355 — below the settlement — as buyers hold back and traders arbitrage spot vs contract. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a softening tape.

Platts duty-unpaid last marked $525–560 (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul, down $70 vs mid-May as the war-spike unwinds; SMM's weekly flags Europe easing with all regions. The 6% EU duty, CBAM carbon costs and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor.

SMM's weekly DDP print eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul, from 110.25¢); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,578 at the 10-Jul LME cash $3,156) dominates the all-in value — easing tracks policy arithmetic and off-season demand, not physical loosening alone.

The decomposition below separates the US all-in premium into the tariff component (rate × LME) and the residual market premium — policy is roughly two-thirds of the US value today, which is why US conversion economics track Washington as much as the physical market.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 is settled at $395 (Platts, decade high) but spot printed $355 — BELOW the settlement — before the weekend. Saturday's strait-closure declaration is the one development that can arrest the slide: if the Muscat mechanism (or an Iranian open-strait statement) lands, Q4 talks (~Sep) open in the LOWER half of the $340–410 band; if closure risk persists, the decade-high anchor holds and spot re-rates back toward it.

European (EDU): last Platts mark $525–560 (03-Jul) with SMM flagging broad softness since; the 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's care & maintenance keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war — and the marginal driver (war-risk freight) flipped from easing to RE-WIDENING over the weekend.

US Midwest: SMM DDP eased to 109.75¢/lb (10-Jul); the tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,578) still dominates ~2/3 of the all-in value, so US premia track the LME and Washington. A Hormuz-driven LME gap mechanically LIFTS the US all-in even with physical demand in its off-season — watch the tariff arithmetic, not the spot survey, for Monday's move.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: the surplus DEEPENS — EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul (~50% within days, full capability end-2026), removing the last supply-side tail-risk premium; expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$340 easing, API ~$304, Platts futures ~$309) with SHFE AO below all MAs and port stocks building. Guinea's pending quota policy is the only clear bullish catalyst.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments (Metro Mining 1.8 Mt in Q2) add length; EGA's refinery ramp is explicitly 'subject to optimisation of bauxite supply chains' — its buying returns demand to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push (the key cost-curve risk).

Caustic soda / lime: stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia caustic); Gulf war-risk surcharges on reagent freight had been easing on the stand-down — Saturday's ship attack points them back UP until the Muscat safe-passage mechanism is confirmed.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,454.00 (10-Jul official, +0.3% — holding firm with the complex through the escalation headlines); busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Fri 10-Jul print: 2,944 (+34) — a fresh one-month high after Thursday's 2,910; Capesize momentum intact. Dry-bulk freight for bauxite/alumina keeps rising on DEMAND; the war-risk component now re-prices separately after Saturday's ship attack (Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News).

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: Saturday: the IRGC fired on / hit a merchant ship attempting the transit and declared the waterway closed; the US struck dozens of targets in response. Muscat talks (Araghchi–Albusaidi) continue on a safe-passage mechanism, and Washington expects Tehran to declare the strait open — until that statement lands, treat transit as case-by-case and expect carriers/insurers to hold back. ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use Hormuz (CNN / WaPo / The National, 11-Jul).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk premia had been stabilising on the stand-down — Saturday's attack on a commercial vessel is precisely the trigger that re-widens quotes and re-activates exclusion clauses. Expect fresh surcharges and slower quote validity until the Muscat mechanism (or an Iranian open-strait statement) is confirmed.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: The case for trucking-workaround readiness re-strengthened over the weekend: keep carrier capacity and costings current and re-validate now rather than mid-crisis — the strait just went from 'reopening' to 'declared closed' in 24 hours.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Renewed closure risk revives demurrage and force-majeure exposure on Gulf-loading contracts: re-check FM language, laycan flexibility and CIF/FOB optionality before Monday fixtures; renegotiating terms is materially harder once quotes re-widen.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — Brent settled $76.01 Friday, +4% w/w, with weekend gap-risk; EU TTF still elevated), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,454 Friday official, +0.3%).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics: freight is rising on DEMAND (BDI 2,944 Friday, +34; one-month high) AND war risk is re-widening after Saturday's ship attack — price the two components separately; alumina buyers keep leverage on the commodity leg (EGA restart) while the delivered-cost leg deteriorates.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at $3,855 (02-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and the ABC correction that followed (A $3,359 16-Jun, B $3,406 22-Jun, C $3,061.5 02-Jul) landed inside the $2,950–3,080 confluence (.50 retracement / prior 4th-of-lesser-degree), where corrections of this degree typically end. The recovery off that low has been three-steps-forward, one-back: five official up-days, then Friday's rejection (official $3,153, close $3,139.5) — and Saturday's re-escalation now supplies the fundamental spark the count was waiting for. Base case (50%): the corrective low is IN at $3,061.5 and a multi-month recovery builds toward $3,400–3,600 while weekly closes hold $3,050. Bull alternate (25%, up from 15%): a sustained strait closure accelerates price through $3,260 (.382) toward a $3,700+ retest without the normal basing work. Bear alternate (25%): the Muscat track delivers a durable safe-passage deal and supply repair resumes control — a final probe of the .618 at $2,894 before the larger recovery. Invalidation: a weekly close below $2,850 voids the corrective count and re-opens $2,600. Confidence: moderate — structure constructive, and the backwardation flip adds physical confirmation the price low may already be behind us.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Daily (swing): from the $3,855 top, wave A fell to $3,359 (16-Jun), wave B was sold at the $3,607 spike (22-Jun), and wave C unwound to $3,065 3M (02-Jul) — a textbook tag of the .50 retracement ($3,077). The bounce ran five official up-days to $3,169, peaked at Thursday evening's $3,210.5 (+2.29%), and Friday delivered the wave-ii pullback: official $3,153, closing print $3,139.5 — a near-exact tag of the .50 of the $3,065→$3,210.5 advance ($3,138), with the .618 at $3,121 just below. That zone is where wave-ii's typically end — and the weekend's escalation (strait declared closed, US strikes) is a textbook wave-iii catalyst arriving on schedule. Base case (45%): ii completed (or completes early week) in $3,120–150 and wave iii presses through MA20 (~$3,230) to $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline), whose daily close confirms the correction over and opens $3,400. Bull alternate (30%, up from 20%): Monday gaps through $3,260 directly on the closure headlines — chase-risk high, structure unchanged. Bear alternate (25%): the Muscat mechanism lands fast, the gap fades, and a daily close below $3,065 re-opens $2,990–3,020 (then $2,894). Invalidation for the base: a daily close below $3,050. Confidence: moderate-high — price, spread structure (backwardation flip) and the news cycle now point the same way; the risk is headline whipsaw, not the count.

premium_drivers.d: Saturday's IRGC attack on a merchant transit + declared strait closure directly threatens ~9% of global / ~25% of ex-China supply routed through the Gulf — the one force that re-floats Asian and EU premiums after last week's broad slide; an Iranian 'strait open' statement is the reversal trigger.

The tariff-$ leg ≈ $1,578/t at the 10-Jul LME cash ($3,156 × 50%) — roughly two-thirds of the ~$2,420 US Midwest DDP all-in; US premia mechanically track the LME and Washington more than physical tightness.
src: Federal Register — Adjusting Imports of Aluminum (50% proclamation)

premium_drivers.t: EU: 6% duty + CBAM + lost regional supply
premium_drivers.d: The 6% import duty, CBAM carbon costs and Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance keep the structural floor under Rotterdam premia even as the war-spike unwinds; EDU last marked $525–560 (Platts, 03-Jul).

premium_drivers.t: Freight: demand-led dry bulk + re-widening war risk
premium_drivers.d: BDI 2,944 (+34 Friday, one-month high) raises the base freight leg into premium CIF marks; Saturday's ship attack re-widens Gulf war-risk surcharges that had been stabilising — two separate cost components, both now pointing up.

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP assessment $395/t (06-Jul-2026) via IndexBox; settlements first reported by Bloomberg via Mining.com (03-Jul)

so_what.line: The Gulf flashpoint is back: Iran fired on a merchant ship in the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday and declared the strait closed, drawing fresh US strikes — and Friday's LME officials had already flipped the cash–3M spread into backwardation — so the week opens with gap-up risk on a market that spent ten days pricing peace.
points: Friday's officials snapped the five-day winning streak only gently (cash $3,156, −0.25%; 3M $3,153, −0.50%) — but the signal was underneath: the front spread flipped from −$5 contango to +$3 BACKWARDATION and LME stock fell again to 287,725 t (−43.5% YTD). Flat price softer, physical tighter.

Saturday re-escalated fast: the IRGC hit a ship transiting Hormuz and declared the waterway closed; the US answered with strikes on dozens of targets while talks continued in Muscat on a safe-passage mechanism. Washington now ties any wider negotiation to free tanker transit — an Iranian statement declaring the strait open is the de-escalation trigger to watch; absent that, the war premium re-prices Monday.

The physical market had been leaning the other way: every overseas spot premium fell last week (Japan spot $355 vs the $395 Q3 settlement; US Midwest 109.75¢/lb) while China destocked at a four-year-high pace. Renewed Gulf risk would re-float premiums; a durable stand-down extends the slide — the next weekly prints (Fri) will show which force wins.

Watch this week: Monday's LME/SHFE reopen plus China June trade data; US June CPI (Tue 14-Jul); the US Treasury wind-down deadline on Iranian oil (17-Jul); and the Q2 openers — Alcoa Thursday 16-Jul (after NYSE close), Rio Tinto Q2 production mid-week, Norsk Hydro 22-Jul.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
